narrative:
Jeanne Shaheen is the only person in American history to be elected both governor and U.S. senator of New Hampshire, and the first woman to hold either office in the state [1]. That historic resume makes it all the more fascinating that her Senate career has become a case study in how defense contractor money shapes foreign policy instincts. Five defense contractors sit in her top twenty donors—BAE Systems, Raytheon, Northrop Grumman, Sierra Nevada, and Honeywell—with General Atomics, General Dynamics, and Lockheed Martin rounding out her top hundred [2]. She is the number-one Senate recipient of BAE Systems donations and number two for Sierra Nevada Corp [2].

This is not coincidental. Shaheen sits on the Armed Services, Foreign Relations, and Appropriations Committees—a triple-crown of defense spending influence—and ranks among the top recipients of defense industry cash on all three [2][3]. When the Afghanistan withdrawal approached, Shaheen was one of the loudest voices urging a continued military presence, a position that aligned neatly with the interests of the contractors filling her campaign coffers [4]. She later supported a commission to study the war, though one might argue the $2.3 trillion price tag spoke for itself [5].

To be fair, Shaheen's legislative record contains genuine bright spots relevant to the 80% Bill. She introduced the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act alongside Senators Kelly and Ossoff [6]. She cosponsored the FAMILY Act for universal paid family and medical leave [7] and the DISCLOSE Act to force dark-money donor transparency [8]. She co-introduced Medicare drug price negotiation expansion with Senator Hassan [9]. Her overall source of funds shows 58 percent from large individual donors, 27 percent from small donors, and about 13 percent from PACs [10]—numbers that are unremarkable for a senior senator but notable given that her top PAC contributor is Emily's List at $148,426 [10].

Shaheen announced in March 2025 that she will not seek re-election [1], which means her remaining time in office is liberated from the fundraising treadmill that has defined so much of her career. The question now is whether a senator who no longer needs defense contractor checks will finally vote like someone who doesn't.

David Schweikert has represented the affluent suburbs of Scottsdale and north Phoenix since 2011, currently serving Arizona's 1st Congressional District. He sits on the powerful Ways and Means Committee, where he chairs the Oversight Subcommittee and holds seats on the Tax and Social Security panels [1]. A founding member of the Freedom Caucus — which he quietly left in 2023 — Schweikert has built his brand around fiscal responsibility, technological innovation, and the urgent need to address the national debt [2]. He is also the Republican co-chair of the Congressional Blockchain Caucus, because apparently the man who admitted to 11 ethics violations believes he is the right person to regulate the future of finance [3].

About those ethics violations: in 2020, the House voted on a bipartisan basis to formally reprimand Schweikert after he admitted to misusing his congressional allowance for personal expenses, pressuring staff to do campaign work on official time, and filing campaign finance reports that included a fictitious $100,000 loan to cover up cash discrepancies [4]. He paid a $50,000 fine to the House and later got hit with an additional $125,000 federal fine for campaign finance violations [5]. This is the man who then votes against virtually every transparency and anti-corruption measure in the 80% Bill. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which empowered Medicare to negotiate drug prices and capped insulin at $35 for seniors — though he did support a Republican alternative that capped out-of-pocket costs without the negotiation mechanism [6]. He has voted 12 times to cut Social Security or Medicare, scores a flat zero percent with the Alliance for Retired Americans, and has openly mused about partial privatization of Social Security through market investments [7]. He voted against releasing the Epstein files, prompting constituent outrage and a local editorial demanding he explain himself [8].

On the 80% Bill's anti-corruption provisions, Schweikert is a study in contradictions. He did sign the U.S. Term Limits pledge, which is one of the few areas of alignment [9]. But he voted for legislation that would let banks and credit card companies increase junk fees on consumers [10], voted to extend Trump-era tax cuts that direct $3.7 trillion in savings to the wealthiest 10 percent of households while adding trillions to the deficit he claims to lose sleep over [11], and has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, or passing the DISCLOSE Act. On the carried interest loophole — a favorite of the private equity donors who populate his contributor lists — Schweikert has been conspicuously silent even as his own party's leader called for closing it [11].

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Schweikert's top industry contributors over his career come from the securities and investment, real estate, and insurance sectors — precisely the industries that benefit most from the tax loopholes and deregulation he champions from his Ways and Means perch [12]. The finance, insurance, and real estate sector gave over $82 million to federal candidates in 2024, and Schweikert has been a reliable recipient [13]. His leadership PAC, Friends of David Schweikert, funnels corporate money through the same networks he was fined for abusing [14]. For a man who made the national debt his "top issue" in a Washington Post profile, his willingness to vote for an "immoral" budget that subsidizes billionaire tax cuts — his own word, "immoral," before he voted yes anyway — tells you everything about where fiscal responsibility ranks when the donor class comes calling [11].

Schweikert is, in short, the 80% Bill's natural antagonist: a congressman who talks about accountability while racking up ethics fines, preaches fiscal discipline while voting for trillions in unfunded tax cuts, and positions himself as a tech-forward innovator while blocking the transparency measures that 86 percent of Americans support. His constituents in Scottsdale deserve to know that the only reform he has signed onto is term limits — and given his 15 years in office, one suspects even that pledge comes with an asterisk.

Tim Scott, the senior senator from South Carolina and chairman of the Senate Banking Committee, has built a career on the premise that Wall Street knows best — especially when Wall Street is writing the checks. As the self-proclaimed champion of "Opportunity Zones," Scott authored a tax break so generous to hedge funds and real estate developers that over half the benefits flowed to just 1% of the designated zones, mostly financing luxury condos. He then collected hundreds of thousands of dollars from wealthy donors who stood to profit from his creation, including NFL franchise owners and the chairman of New Balance. It's not a conflict of interest if you call it "economic empowerment."

As Banking Committee chair, Scott has declared crypto "the next wonder of the world" and met privately with executives from Coinbase, Kraken, and Ripple to craft industry-friendly regulation. His idea of oversight is asking the fox how the henhouse should be designed. Meanwhile, he's never met a stock disclosure he couldn't forget about — nearly a dozen stocks appeared on his 2022 financial disclosure that were mysteriously absent from 2021, with no purchase reports filed as required by the STOCK Act. His campaign insists he "takes his compliance obligation seriously," which is apparently code for "takes it casually."

On the 80% Bill's priorities, Scott is a reliable opponent. He voted against the DISCLOSE Act to unmask dark money, he's rated 0% by the Alliance for Retired Americans for his pro-privatization stance on Social Security and Medicare, and he voted to raise the Medicare eligibility age to 70. Drug price negotiation? He co-signed pharmaceutical industry letters opposing it. Congressional stock trading ban? He failed to properly disclose his own trades. Term limits? He's been in Congress since 2011 with no plans to self-impose any.

Scott has raised millions from the securities and investment industry, real estate interests, and corporate PACs — the very sectors his Banking Committee oversees. Credit One Bank alone gave $74,700 to his Victory Fund in the 2024 cycle. When your top donors are the companies you regulate, "financial inclusion" starts to sound like a euphemism for "regulatory capture." South Carolinians deserve a senator whose opportunity zones aren't limited to his donor list.

Rep. Terri Sewell has represented Alabama's 7th Congressional District since 2011, making her the first Black woman ever elected to Congress from Alabama and now a 14-year incumbent. Raised in Selma in the shadow of the Edmund Pettus Bridge — ground zero of the Voting Rights Movement — she graduated valedictorian of Selma High School before earning degrees from Princeton, Oxford, and Harvard Law[1][2]. Before Congress, she spent over 15 years as a securities and public finance attorney, becoming the first Black woman partner at the Birmingham office of Maynard, Cooper & Gale[2]. She now sits on the powerful House Ways and Means Committee — the panel that writes the nation's tax and healthcare law[2]. It is a biography that practically writes its own irony: a civil rights icon's district, represented by someone whose professional formation was in Wall Street-adjacent law, underwriting the very financial structures her constituents have historically been locked out of.

On issues aligned with the 80% Bill, Sewell's record tilts progressive on rhetoric and patchy on follow-through. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act — the only Alabama member to do so — which unlocked Medicare drug price negotiation for the first time[3]. She is the lead House sponsor of the John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act, reintroducing it again in 2025, and cosponsored the Freedom to Vote Act, which would ban dark money, end partisan gerrymandering, and require donor disclosure[4][5]. She has been an original cosponsor of the Social Security 2100 Act and serves as co-chair of the Expand Social Security Caucus[6]. She cosponsored a bipartisan paid family leave bill going back to 2017[7]. She opposed the 2025 Republican tax bill with notable fervor — declaring herself a "HELL NO" at markup — arguing it handed "$4 trillion worth of tax giveaways to the wealthy"[8].

The contradictions surface sharply when the subject is congressional stock trading. In 2025, while publicly backing the Democratic "Restore Trust in Government Act" to ban congressional stock ownership, Sewell simultaneously ranked as the third most profitable stock trader in Congress, generating a 67.9% return — nearly four times the S&P 500's 16.8% return — by purchasing Apple and Nvidia shares[9][10]. Her spokesperson insists she "takes extremely seriously her role as a steward of the public trust." Congress has not passed a stock trading ban. She keeps trading.

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. During the 2018 cycle, Sludge identified Sewell as the House Democrat most reliant on corporate PAC money: 74.6% of her campaign fundraising — over $1.3 million — came from corporate PACs, with top donors including Blue Cross Blue Shield, Morgan Stanley, AT&T, and Lockheed Martin[11]. Small donors giving under $200 accounted for just 0.86% of her haul[11]. Her career industry donors are dominated by insurance, securities and investments, and commercial banks[12] — the precise industries regulated by the Ways and Means Committee on which she sits. She cosponsors bills to ban dark money and disclose donor identity; she does not appear to have cosponsored the Ban Corporate PACs Act herself[13]. Terri Sewell is not a villain, and for the most impoverished congressional district in one of America's poorest states — where all 25 Black Belt counties rank among the 35 highest-poverty counties in the nation[14] — she has delivered on voting rights, drug pricing, and Social Security in ways that materially matter. But a lawmaker who can denounce billionaire tax giveaways with genuine fire in the morning and post a 67.9% stock return by evening deserves the same scrutiny her floor speeches demand of everyone else.

Marlin Stutzman is a fourth-generation Indiana farmer who rode the Tea Party wave into Congress in 2010, lost a Senate primary to Todd Young in 2016, spent seven years in the political wilderness, and then returned to the House in 2025 to represent Indiana's 3rd District. A member of the House Freedom Caucus during his first stint in Washington, Stutzman built a reputation as a fiscal conservative willing to shut down the government on principle — or at least on something. During the 2013 government shutdown, he delivered what may be the most accidentally honest quote of the Obama era: "We have to get something out of this. And I don't know what that even is" [1]. He walked it back the next day, but the damage was done. Now serving on the House Financial Services Committee in the 119th Congress, Stutzman has returned to Washington older, presumably wiser, and just as eager to cut spending that does not flow to agriculture [2].

On the twenty issues where roughly eighty percent of Americans agree, Stutzman's record is a revealing patchwork. He has sponsored legislation to restrict the sale of federally financed single-family homes to corporate buyers, a populist move that aligns neatly with the 80% Bill's call to ban corporate purchases of single-family homes [3]. He has historically supported congressional term limits, a cost-free position for a man who already lost his Senate bid. But on the meatier reforms — banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, closing the carried interest loophole, auditing the Pentagon — Stutzman has been conspicuously silent. He opposed the Affordable Care Act with the fervor of a revival preacher but has never lifted a finger for Medicare drug price negotiation, which would actually lower costs for his constituents [4]. He has shown no interest in the DISCLOSE Act, paid family leave, or any campaign finance reform that might inconvenience his donors.

The farm subsidies saga is where Stutzman's brand of conservatism gets its most entertaining stress test. As a farmer, Stutzman personally received nearly $200,000 in federal farm subsidies over the years. When confronted about this during his first tenure, he told the Fort Wayne Journal Gazette that farmers "have to take" the subsidies — a claim the USDA promptly contradicted by noting the program is entirely voluntary [5]. This did not stop him from co-authoring legislation with Senator John Thune to cut $30 billion from SNAP over a decade, arguing that food assistance had "outgrown its original mission of providing temporary assistance" [6]. The irony of a subsidy recipient lecturing food stamp recipients about dependency was not lost on his critics, though it sailed comfortably over the heads of most primary voters.

Stutzman's campaign finances during his earlier tenure reflected the standard Freedom Caucus funding model: heavy support from the Club for Growth and the Senate Conservatives Fund, supplemented by agricultural interests and small-dollar conservative donors [7]. His 2016 Senate primary against Todd Young became a proxy war between the anti-establishment right and Mitch McConnell's machine — McConnell's allies spent heavily to stop Stutzman, casting him as a Cruz-style disruptor [8]. Now back in the House and on Financial Services, he is positioned to influence banking and housing policy. His Regulation A+ Improvement Act, which raises offering limits for small-business investment, is a genuine pro-business measure, though it also happens to be the kind of deregulation that the financial services industry's PACs reward handsomely [9].

The bottom line on Stutzman is that he is a man who sincerely believes the government spends too much money — on other people. Farm subsidies for Marlin Stutzman are a mandate he cannot escape; food assistance for a single mother in Fort Wayne is a dependency trap that must be reformed. He will fight for term limits that would not have applied to him and sponsor housing bills that sound populist while dodging every reform that might threaten the flow of donor money. He is, in short, a perfectly representative representative — just not of the eighty percent.

Austin Scott has represented Georgia's 8th Congressional District since 2011, a sprawling stretch of south-central Georgia that includes two Air Force bases — Robins and Moody — and mile after mile of farmland. Before Congress he spent nearly two decades running an insurance brokerage in Tifton and served fourteen years in the Georgia state legislature, where he chaired the Governmental Affairs Committee [1]. He is now Vice Chairman of the House Agriculture Committee, Chairman of the Subcommittee on General Farm Commodities, and a senior member of the House Armed Services Committee, where he also sits on the Intelligence and Special Operations subcommittee [2]. In October 2023 he briefly ran for Speaker of the House against Jim Jordan, earning 81 votes before dropping out and endorsing Jordan — then jumping back in a week later when Jordan failed on the floor [3]. The man does not lack for confidence.

On the twenty items in the 80% Bill, Scott's record is largely one of silence. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, end dark money, impose term limits, ban corporate PACs, or create a lifetime lobbying ban. He has not backed the DISCLOSE Act or any constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United. He has not signed on to paid family leave, Medicare drug negotiation, junk fee bans, or right-to-repair bills. He did, however, cosponsor H.J.Res. 8, proposing a constitutional amendment to fix the Supreme Court at nine justices [4] — a reform nobody asked for that conveniently sidesteps every structural reform people actually want. On defense spending and Pentagon accountability, Scott has been a reliable advocate for more money rather than more oversight, routinely securing hundreds of millions in funding for Robins Air Force Base through the annual NDAA [5]. Auditing the Pentagon is not on his to-do list when the Pentagon is writing checks to his district.

The farm bill is where Scott does his most visible work, and it is also where the contradictions live. He has pushed to "put the farm back in the Farm Bill" by separating SNAP nutrition benefits from agricultural policy, arguing that "no country has enough money to take care of people who won't work" [6]. He brokered billions in federal disaster relief for Georgia farmers hit by hurricanes [7]. Yet his committee seat also puts him squarely in the orbit of agribusiness PACs and crop insurance interests — the very industry he spent his pre-congressional career working in. His estimated net worth of $1.6 million is modest by congressional standards, but Quiver Quantitative records show he has actively traded stocks while in office, including a nearly 800% return on FuelCell Energy shares bought in 2020 and sold in 2021 [8]. For a member who has not cosponsored a stock trading ban, the trades speak louder than any position paper.

Scott is a conservative pragmatist in a safe red seat, which means he faces no electoral pressure to embrace popular reforms on dark money, lobbying, or corporate influence. His district wants defense dollars flowing to Robins and Moody, crop insurance protections for farmers, and a congressman who will not rock the boat. Scott delivers exactly that. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives align with the supermajority preferences of the American public on twenty broadly popular issues. On that scorecard, Austin Scott is a case study in how a member can serve his district's institutional interests — the bases, the farms, the insurance industry — while ignoring the structural reforms that poll at 70 or 80 percent nationwide. The voters of Georgia's 8th may not mind, but the rest of the country is paying for it.

Brad Schneider has represented Illinois's 10th Congressional District since 2017, having first won the seat in 2012, lost it in 2014, and won it back two years later with the persistence of a man who really, really wanted to commute from the North Shore suburbs to Washington [1]. A former management consultant and business strategist, Schneider serves on the Ways and Means Committee — one of the most powerful perches in the House and, not coincidentally, one of the most lucrative for fundraising [2]. His district hugs the Lake Michigan shoreline north of Chicago, running through affluent communities like Highland Park, Deerfield, and Northbrook. It is the kind of place where voters describe themselves as socially liberal and fiscally responsible, which in practice means they want good schools and low capital gains taxes.

On the 80% Bill issues, Schneider checks many of the expected Democratic boxes while leaving some conspicuous gaps. He cosponsored the STOCK Act improvements and has supported a ban on congressional stock trading [3]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation — the first time the federal government was allowed to bargain with pharmaceutical companies on behalf of seniors [4]. He has backed the DISCLOSE Act to require transparency in campaign spending and cosponsored legislation to ban junk fees [5][6]. He supports paid family leave, has advocated for police body camera funding, and has spoken in favor of protecting Social Security's solvency [7]. On paper, this looks like a reformer's résumé.

But the gaps tell their own story. Schneider has not cosponsored a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United, despite representing a district where campaign finance reform polls exceptionally well [8]. He has been notably quiet on congressional term limits — understandable, perhaps, for a man who fought to get his seat back after losing it and clearly intends to keep it indefinitely. He has not championed a lifetime lobbying ban, auditing the Pentagon, or banning corporate purchases of single-family homes. On ending the post-9/11 Authorizations for Use of Military Force, Schneider has been cautious, voting to repeal the 2002 Iraq AUMF but hedging on broader repeal efforts, a posture shaped by his position as a strong supporter of U.S.-Israel policy who worries about constraining executive authority in the Middle East [9].

The fundraising picture fills in the blanks. Schneider has been one of the more prolific fundraisers among House Democrats, raising over $4 million in the 2024 cycle [10]. His top industry donors include securities and investment firms, real estate, and the insurance industry [11]. Pro-Israel organizations, including AIPAC's affiliated PAC, have been among his most significant backers [12]. Corporate PACs from the financial sector contribute generously to a member of Ways and Means who handles tax policy — a dynamic that might explain his lack of enthusiasm for closing the carried interest loophole, despite it being one of the most broadly popular tax reforms in America. Schneider is not hostile to reform; he simply represents the wing of the Democratic Party that believes in incremental progress at a pace that never quite threatens the donor class.

Schneider is, in many ways, the archetypal suburban Democrat — polished, credentialed, and genuinely committed to a set of issues that happen to align comfortably with what his affluent constituents and major donors want. He will fight for drug pricing reform and disclosure requirements, but do not expect him to set fire to the system that elevated him. In a district that was shaken by the 2022 Highland Park mass shooting, he has been a forceful advocate for gun safety legislation, and that work is real and consequential [13]. But on the structural reforms that would redistribute power away from the consultant class and toward ordinary voters — term limits, dark money bans, lobbying restrictions — Schneider moves with the caution of a man who has built his career inside the machine and sees no reason to dismantle it.

Eric Swalwell has been in Congress since 2013, when he pulled off the unlikely feat of unseating 40-year incumbent Pete Stark in California's East Bay. A former Alameda County prosecutor who once led the Hate Crimes Unit, Swalwell built a brand around youth, energy, and gun control -- launching a quixotic presidential bid in 2019 that lasted roughly as long as a Snapchat story [1][2]. Now representing CA-14, he sits on the Judiciary and Homeland Security committees and serves as Ranking Member of the Cybersecurity subcommittee [3]. But his attention has drifted: in November 2025 he announced a run for California governor, and promptly missed 82% of House votes over a two-month stretch [4][5].

On the 80% Bill's priorities, Swalwell checks many of the right boxes -- in rhetoric, at least. He co-sponsored H.R. 1, the For the People Act, which bundled together dark money disclosure, anti-gerrymandering provisions, and campaign finance reform [6]. He supports a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United and has called for Medicare for All, writing that it is "the obvious solution to a health care system that still drives people to financial ruin" [7][8]. He pushed for Congress to debate new war authorizations, arguing the existing AUMFs are "out of date" [9]. On the Epstein files, he was vocal and effective, predicting a Republican "jailbreak" of over 100 members supporting the transparency bill and demanding hearings when the Trump administration slow-walked document releases [10][11]. He has also advocated for paid family leave and introduced housing affordability legislation [12][13].

The contradictions, however, are illuminating. Swalwell pledged during his presidential campaign to reject corporate PAC money -- a noble gesture somewhat undermined by the $2.7 million in PAC contributions he had already collected over his career, with $1.9 million of that coming from business PACs [14]. His top historical donors include Calpine Corporation (natural gas), PG&E, Intel, and the National Association of Realtors [14][15]. For the 2024 cycle, he raised over $4.1 million, with $628,063 from PAC and party money [15]. It is the posture of a man who quits smoking after finishing the carton.

Then there is the spending. Swalwell's campaign burned through nearly $583,000 on travel in a single cycle -- more than Nancy Pelosi -- including $28,646 on yacht charters, $4,708 at Loews Miami Beach, $1,800 at a five-star Paris hotel, and $131,000 in childcare expenses that drew an FEC complaint [16][17]. The Fang Fang episode, in which a suspected Chinese intelligence operative bundled donations and placed an intern in his office before the FBI intervened, ended without charges but left a permanent footnote on his record [18][19]. The House Ethics Committee closed its investigation in 2023, taking no action [19].

Swalwell talks a good game on the 80% Bill's agenda, and his legislative record contains genuine efforts on war powers, campaign finance reform, and consumer protection. But the gap between his populist messaging and his campaign finance reality -- corporate PAC millions, luxury travel funded by donors, and a gubernatorial pivot that has him literally phoning it in on House votes -- suggests a politician who has always been more interested in the next office than the current one.

Brian Schatz has represented Hawaii in the United States Senate since 2012, when Governor Neil Abercrombie appointed him to fill the seat left vacant by the death of Daniel Inouye. He won a special election in 2014 and full terms in 2016 and 2022, each time by comfortable margins befitting a deep-blue state where the biggest electoral threat is a primary challenge, not a Republican [1]. Schatz sits on the Appropriations Committee, the Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee, the Foreign Relations Committee, and serves as Vice Chairman of the Indian Affairs Committee [2]. He is, by most conventional scorecards, a reliable progressive -- which makes him a useful test case for whether "reliable progressive" and "aligned with what eighty percent of Americans actually want" are the same thing.

On the populist-reform planks of the 80% Bill, Schatz checks a remarkable number of boxes. He cosponsored the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act and, for his part, holds no individual stocks, keeping his assets in index funds, retirement accounts, and real estate [3][4]. He introduced the SAFE Social Security Act to expand benefits and lift the payroll tax wage cap [5]. He authored the Police CAMERA Act with Rand Paul -- one of the more genuinely bipartisan pairings in recent memory -- to fund body camera programs for law enforcement [6]. He cosponsored the Carried Interest Fairness Act to close the hedge-fund manager tax loophole and introduced the Wall Street Tax Act to impose a 0.1% financial transaction tax [7][8]. He has been a vocal proponent of paid family leave, introducing the Federal Employees Paid Leave Act to guarantee twelve weeks of paid leave for federal workers [9]. And he has loudly demanded the release of the Epstein files, tweeting that the House shutting down to avoid a vote on the matter "should cause everyone to recalibrate their conspiracy meter from like a 6 out of 10 to 8.5 out of 10" [10].

On campaign finance, Schatz cosponsored the Democracy for All Amendment to overturn Citizens United and backed the DISCLOSE Act [11]. He joined Sanders and Schakowsky in introducing legislation to prevent corporate tax dodging [12]. He has supported the Protecting America's Agricultural Land from Foreign Harm Act, which would bar adversary nations from purchasing U.S. farmland [13]. On war powers, he has generally supported efforts to reassert congressional authority, though he has not been a lead sponsor on AUMF repeal legislation [14]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the first-ever Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, and had long criticized the prohibition on Medicare negotiating drug prices [15].

Follow the money and you find relatively little scandal. Schatz raised approximately $7.5 million for his 2022 reelection, with top contributing industries including lawyers and law firms, securities and investment, and real estate [16]. His top organizational contributors tend to be large law firms and financial services companies -- the kind of donors who give to virtually every Appropriations Committee member regardless of party [17]. He does not appear to accept corporate PAC money in significant quantities, and his personal financial disclosures are notably boring by Senate standards, with no individual stock trades to raise eyebrows [4]. For a senator sitting on the Defense Appropriations subcommittee, that restraint is at least noteworthy.

The bottom line on Schatz is that he is one of those rare members who lines up with a supermajority of the 80% Bill agenda without obvious contradictions between his positions and his donors. He is not flashy about it -- he does not have the brand-building instincts of a Sanders or a Warren -- but on stock trading, Social Security, body cameras, tax fairness, campaign finance reform, paid leave, and the Epstein files, his record is substantive and consistent. Whether that makes him a model legislator or merely proof that alignment is easier when you represent one of the safest seats in America is a question worth sitting with.

Jason Smith is a seventh-generation Missouri farmer turned congressman who has represented the state's 8th district since 2013. At 25, he became the youngest member of the Missouri House of Representatives, and he has since parlayed that early ambition into one of the most powerful seats in Washington: chairman of the House Ways and Means Committee, the oldest committee in Congress and the one that writes the nation's tax code [1]. He likes to remind people he grew up on a family farm. He does not like to remind people that he now receives more corporate PAC money than any House member in at least two decades [2].

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Smith's record reads like a checklist of popular proposals he has methodically avoided or opposed. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a provision supported by nearly 90% of Americans -- and then had the audacity to claim the Biden administration was "pricing seniors out of their health care" [3][4]. As chairman of the committee overseeing Social Security, he told Axios that "everything is on the table" regarding cuts to the program, and in 2014 he voted to raise the retirement age to 70 [5]. He has not cosponsored the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act, the DISCLOSE Act, or any legislation to ban corporate PACs or dark money [6][7]. On term limits, lobbying bans, and Pentagon audits, his position is best described as a deafening silence -- the kind you can only afford when your seat is safe enough to ignore your constituents.

Smith has shown no interest in closing the tax loopholes that benefit his wealthiest donors. Instead, his signature priority has been making the 2017 Trump tax cuts permanent -- cuts that disproportionately benefited corporations and the ultra-wealthy [8]. He has fought to preserve Section 199A, the pass-through deduction that primarily benefits high-income business owners, while framing it as relief for "small businesses" [9]. On fair elections, ending gerrymandering, and banning junk fees, Smith has been conspicuously absent from any reform effort.

Follow the money and the silence makes sense. In the 2024 cycle, Smith hauled in $3.3 million from business PACs -- more than any House member in any cycle since at least 2002, according to OpenSecrets [2]. His donor rolodex reads like a Fortune 500 directory: AT&T, Duke Energy, Bank of America, Google, and Anheuser-Busch all cut him maximum $5,000 checks [2]. After becoming Ways and Means chairman in 2023, his quarterly fundraising exploded from $76,088 to over $1 million, with PAC donations surging from $16,000 to $618,000 in a single quarter [10]. The industries filling his coffers -- insurance, oil and gas, pharmaceuticals, defense contractors, and realtors -- are precisely the ones that benefit from the tax loopholes he refuses to close [11].

The bottom line is this: Jason Smith represents a district where the median household income is roughly $43,000, but he legislates like a man whose real constituents wire money from K Street. He chairs the committee that could close tax loopholes, negotiate drug prices, and fund Social Security for generations -- and instead, he uses it to ensure the corporate checks keep clearing.
